---
title: "A Review of Naturally Occurring Food Allergens and Their Impact on Health"
author: "Saraswathi HTB, Chittoor R.I."
year: 2022
doi: "DON'T KNOW"
journal: "Biosciences Biotechnology Research Asia"
volume: "19(1)"
url: "https://www.biotech-asia.org/vol19no1/a-review-of-naturally-occuring-food-allergens-and-their-impact-on-health/"
pmid: "DON'T KNOW"
access: "Open access — available at biotech-asia.org. Full text fetched."
source_quality: "Full text fetched from biotech-asia.org. Points marked [FULL TEXT] are from the paper. Note: fetch returned structured AI summary — exact page numbers not available. Quoted text may be approximate."
---

# A Review of Naturally Occurring Food Allergens and Their Impact on Health

**Authors:** Saraswathi HTB, Chittoor R.I. (Department of Life Sciences, University of Calicut, Kerala, India)
**Year:** 2022 (received Dec 2021, accepted Jan 2022, published online Feb 2022)
**DOI:** DON'T KNOW — journal does not appear to use DOIs, or DOI not indexed in standard databases
**Journal:** Biosciences Biotechnology Research Asia, 19(1)
**URL:** https://www.biotech-asia.org/vol19no1/a-review-of-naturally-occuring-food-allergens-and-their-impact-on-health/

## Why this paper

Brought in for India-specific comparison of hypersensitivity rates vs sensitisation rates in children. Note on journal: Biosciences Biotechnology Research Asia is a low-impact Indian journal not indexed in PubMed. This affects the weight we assign to its findings — data this paper cites likely comes from other primary sources, and those primary sources should be identified and cited directly where possible.

## Extracted points

> **Source quality note:** Full text was fetched. Points below came through an AI-mediated summary of the fetched content. Quoted text marked with quotation marks may be approximate. Treat all numbers as requiring verification against the paper before manuscript citation.

1. Food allergy prevalence in India in children: "0.14 % among youngsters." — [FULL TEXT]
   Context: DON'T KNOW which study or dataset this figure is drawn from. This number also appears in P01 (Mahesh 2023a) — likely the same underlying source.
   For us: Consistent with other papers in our set. Need to identify the primary source this figure traces back to.

2. Food allergen sensitisation rate in Indian children: "around 19.1 %." — [FULL TEXT]
   Context: Same caveat — DON'T KNOW primary source. Consistent with P01 and P04.

3. Nut allergy prevalence in Indian children: "low (around 0.03%)." — [FULL TEXT]
   Context: DON'T KNOW primary source. Consistent with P04 (Krishna 2020).

4. Nut sensitisation rate in Indian children: "high at 6.3%." — [FULL TEXT]
   Context: DON'T KNOW primary source. Consistent with P04.

5. Paper highlights this as a paradox requiring investigation: "further examination is expected to explore why predominance of clinically applicable food sensitivity is low in India in spite of high sensitisation rate." — [FULL TEXT — likely a direct quote or close paraphrase]
   For us: This is the second paper in our set (after P04) to explicitly flag this gap. The fact that multiple papers note it without explaining it is itself a finding.

6. Global food allergy impact: "affects over 200 million people." — [FULL TEXT]
   Context: DON'T KNOW source year or methodology.

7. US childhood prevalence increase: "from 3.4% in 1997 to 1999" to "5.1% in 2009 to 2011." — [FULL TEXT]
   Context: US-specific figure, included for comparison.

8. Australia school-age survey: "41% expansion from 2009 to 2014." — [FULL TEXT]
   Context: DON'T KNOW which specific allergens drove this.

9. Major allergens discussed: peanuts, soy, wheat, fish, eggs, shellfish, tree nuts, milk. — [FULL TEXT]
   Context: These appear to be the Big 8/9 framing — the paper does not appear to go beyond this list to India-specific allergens based on the fetch summary.

10. Allergen molecular weight range: "from 10 to 70 kDa." — [FULL TEXT]
    Context: General characterisation of food allergens as proteins in this size range.

11. Allergen protein families mentioned: cupins (seed storage proteins), prolamins (cereal storage proteins), tropomyosin (muscle protein responsible for seafood allergy). — [FULL TEXT]
    For us: Useful for the mechanism section — these families explain cross-reactivity patterns.

12. Limitation: "Current comprehension of the turn of the events and utilisation of allergenic capability of certain types of food proteins is restricted" — [FULL TEXT — direct quote, awkward phrasing suggests translation from another language or unusual register]
    Additional limitation: "no animal models are universally validated for allergen-specific assessment." — [FULL TEXT]

## Assessment

This paper is a narrative review drawing on other sources. The India-specific numbers (0.14%, 19.1%, 0.03%, 6.3%) appear to trace back to the same underlying studies cited in P01 and P04 — not independent data. This paper should not be cited as an independent data source for those figures. Its value is in confirming the consensus framing and the sensitisation-reactivity paradox as a recognised theme.
